coredata.dc:title: A fluorometric sandwich biosensor based on rationally imprinted magnetic particles and aptamer modified carbon dots for the detection of tropomyosin in seafood products
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Yanbo
Li, Linfang
Li, Huan
Peng, Yafei
Fu, Linglin
coredata.dc:description: Tropomyosin (TM), a major allergen in shellfish, has raised increasing concern in industrialized countries and resulted in an urgent need for accurate, simple, and sensitive methods for tracing its presence in food products. Herein, we proposed a sandwich biosensor that employed the rationally imprinted magnetic particles and aptamer modified carbon dots for the detection of TM in seafood products. The multi-point initiated polydopamine was employed as molecular imprinting polymer on magnetic particles, which was subjected to antifouling surface modification to achieve efficient separation of TM. By taking the aptamer modified carbon dots as signal probes, a sandwich assay was achieved with the limit of detection (LOD) being 30.76 ng mL−1 (S/N = 3). The proposed method not only possessed excellent specificity towards TM but exhibited great accuracy. The sandwich biosensor was further used to quantitatively analyze TM in five shrimp and five shrimp processed products, which proved that the proposed biosensor could detect TM in commercial samples and had a great application prospect.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sandwich assay
Molecular imprinting
Aptamer
Carbon dot
Food allergy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Affinity and specificity of the TM-imprinted Fe3O4@PDA
Preparation of the fluorescent CD-APT probes
Detection of TM by the sandwich biosensor
Preparation and optimization of the TM-imprinted Fe3O4@PDA
Affinity and specificity of the imprinted Fe3O4@PDA towards TM
Method validation of the proposed method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the sandwich biosensors for TM. (a) Preparation of the TM-imprinted Fe3O4@PDA, (b) preparation of the fluorescent CD-APT probe, and (c) detection of TM by the sandwich biosensor.

Characterization of the Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@PDA particles. (a) TEM images of the Fe3O4 and Fe3O4@PDA particles. The scale bar of the left column was 100 nm, and the right column was 200 nm. (b) FT-IR spectra of the Fe3O4 (black line) and Fe3O4@PDA (red line) particles. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Affinity and specificity of the imprinted Fe3O4@PDA to TM. (a) Binding isotherms of TM on the control Fe3O4@PDA (black line) and the TM imprinted Fe3O4@PDA (red line). (b) Binding behaviors of the proteins on the control Fe3O4@PDA and the TM imprinted Fe3O4@PDA. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001 compared with TM group. n = 3. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

Optimization of the detection conditions of the sandwich biosensor. (a) Optimization of the ratio of the imprinted Fe3O4@PDA to CD-APT probes. (b) Optimization of the detection temperature. (c) Optimization of the detection time. n = 3.

Detection performance of the sandwich biosensors. (a) The standard curve of the sandwich biosensors. (b) Specificity of the sandwich biosensor. *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, ****p < 0.0001 in comparison to the TM group. n = 3.
Element analysis of the Fe3O4 and imprinted Fe3O4@PDA particles.
Recoveries of the samples spiked with different amount of TM.
Summary of recently reported detection methods for food allergens.
Amounts of TM in commercial samples detected by the sandwich biosensor and ELISA.

head.ce:title: A fluorometric sandwich biosensor based on rationally imprinted magnetic particles and aptamer modified carbon dots for the detection of tropomyosin in seafood products

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Food Safety Key Laboratory of Zhejiang Province, School of Food Science and Biotechnology, Zhejiang Gongshang University, Hangzhou, 310018, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Food Safety Key Laboratory of Zhejiang Province
School of Food Science and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Food Safety Key Laboratory of Zhejiang Province, School of Food Science and Biotechnology, Zhejiang Gongshang University, Hangzhou, 310018, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. 18 Xue Zheng Street, Hangzhou, 310018, Zhejiang Province, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Tropomyosin (TM), a major allergen in shellfish, has raised increasing concern in industrialized countries and resulted in an urgent need for accurate, simple, and sensitive methods for tracing its presence in food products. Herein, we proposed a sandwich biosensor that employed the rationally imprinted magnetic particles and aptamer modified carbon dots for the detection of TM in seafood products. The multi-point initiated polydopamine was employed as molecular imprinting polymer on magnetic particles, which was subjected to antifouling surface modification to achieve efficient separation of TM. By taking the aptamer modified carbon dots as signal probes, a sandwich assay was achieved with the limit of detection (LOD) being 30.76 ng mL−1 (S/N = 3). The proposed method not only possessed excellent specificity towards TM but exhibited great accuracy. The sandwich biosensor was further used to quantitatively analyze TM in five shrimp and five shrimp processed products, which proved that the proposed biosensor could detect TM in commercial samples and had a great application prospect.

ce:section.ce:para: Shellfish is one of the “Big eight” food allergen groups that are responsible for approximately 16.1% of all food allergy cases (Wai et al., 2020). Among the diverse allergens in shellfish, tropomyosin (TM) has been considered the most important one for many years, which is a heat-stable protein with a unique α-helical coiled-coil structure. TM exhibits resistance to digestion and has a molecular weight ranging from 36 to 38 kDa (Zeng et al., 2019). It's been found that approximately 80% of shrimp-allergic patients are allergic to TM (Faber et al., 2017). At this stage, avoiding the ingestion of allergens is still the principal way to control food allergies due to the lack of an effective cure (Eischeid & Stadig, 2018; Lopata et al., 2016). To prevent allergic people from exposure to harmful food allergens, labeling regulation of allergenic ingredients including shellfish has been implemented in a great number of countries (Boden et al., 2005; Koeberl et al., 2018; Shoji et al., 2018; Taylor & Baumert, 2015). Therefore, it's an urgent need to develop a detection method for TM with great accuracy and precision to consummate shellfish allergen management.

Sandwich-type biosensors have witnessed fast development during the last decades (Pei et al., 2013). The principle of the sandwich assay, to recapitulate briefly, is that a recognition probe specifically binds to the analyte to form a complex, whereafter a signal probe recognizes and associates with the analyte in the complex to give out signals that are easily detectable (Piro & Reisberg, 2017). Fluorescent sandwich immunoassay introduces fluorescent signals to monitor the presence and concentration of target analytes (Ho et al., 2008; Rosi & Mirkin, 2005). Compared to the common methods developed for food allergen detection, such as liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (Carrera et al., 2020; Stahl-Zeng et al., 2019), polymerase chain reaction (Jeong & Kim, 2020; Linacero et al., 2020), and indirect competitive immunoassay (Wang et al., 2014), sandwich-type biosensors have the advantage of improved sensitivity and accuracy due to the multi-recognition components (Nakamura et al., 2005; Seo & Gu, 2017). For instance, antibody-based sandwich biosensors have been developed for the detection of the major allergen of mango Man i1 in processed foods, with the detection limit being 21.2 ng mL−1. However, antibodies are expensive and difficult to synthesize (Zhu & Chen, 2018), which hinders the practical application of these biosensors. Therefore, it is of great significance to construct sandwich biosensors with more economical and efficient recognition mechanisms.

Molecular imprinting has been recognized as one of the most promising and efficient techniques for simulating recognition between biological molecules (Chen et al., 2011). It's based on the polymeric spatial structure formed around the template molecules to create “tailor-made’’ binding sites for the template molecule (Roy et al., 2017). Unlike most allergens, TM with α-helical coiled-coil features a rod-like structure (Tsai et al., 2021). This structural characteristic of TM makes molecular imprinting polymer (MIP) to be a promising candidate in developing a specific recognition approach for TM, as it has inherent sensitivity to structures. However, the preservation of the 3D structure of the target analyte in MIP is often hindered by the single-point polymerization (Li et al., 2017), while multi-point polymerization has been reported to be an efficient way to tackle this issue (Xiao et al., 2020). Dopamine that contains catechol and amine groups has been proved to self-polymerize in weakly alkaline media with a multi-point-initiated nature (Lynge et al., 2011; Xia et al., 2013). Moreover, the excellent hydrophilicity and biocompatibility of dopamine make it suitable for imprinting proteins (Bai et al., 2018). Thus, it is suggested to be a promising candidate in constructing molecularly imprinted recognition sites for TM with preserved structural characteristics.

Aptamer (APT) is a short single-stranded oligonucleotide that is often termed as a “chemical antibody”. APT-based probes have been widely applied in constructing biosensors. Owing to the diversity of single-stranded nucleic acid structure and spatial conformation, APT is capable of binding various targets with a high affinity and specificity (Dorraj et al., 2015; Liu et al., 2012). Compared to antibody, APT has many remarkable advantages such as good stability, low cost, reproducible preparation method, and in especial, the ease of labeling and modification (Song et al., 2011; Xi et al., 2014; Zamay et al., 2020; Zhang et al., 2019). Hence, it could be an ideal recognition element for constructing the signal probe in the sandwich biosensor for TM.

Herein, we introduced a novel sandwich biosensor for TM by employing the MIP-based recognition probe and APT-based detection probe. Firstly, a molecularly imprinted polydopamine (PDA) layer was constructed on Fe3O4 magnetic particles to obtain a recognition probe that could directly bind and separate TM from complex food matrices (Fig. 1 a). Magnetic beads such as Fe3O4 particles were found to be able to separate analytes rapidly and thus strongly improved the detection efficiency (Basu & Isaacs, 2019). In addition, a peptide with zwitterionic EKEKEKE moiety was used to modify the non-binding sites of the Fe3O4@PDA to improve the colloidal stability and antifouling ability of the particles. Next, the APT towards TM was conjugated to carbon quantum dot (CD) (Fig. 1b), which was considered to possess excellent fluorescence-based reporting potentials in a sandwich immunoassay (Xiao et al., 2020). After that, a sandwich biosensor was constructed from these two detection probes and further explored for TM detection (Fig. 1c). The sensitivity, accuracy, and specificity of the proposed biosensor were carefully studied. Moreover, it was used to quantitatively analyze TM in five shrimp and five shrimp processed products, which proved that the sandwich biosensor could be applied to the detection of market samples and had a great application prospect.

Ferric trichloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), sodium acetate (CH3COONa), ethylene glycol (C2H6O2), bovine serum albumin (BSA), trimethylol aminomethane (Tris), sodium dodecyl sulfate (SDS), sodium chloride (NaCl), acetic acid (CH3COOH), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), dopamine hydrochloride and Bradford kit were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). EKEKEKEPPPPC polypeptide was purchased from Genscript (Nanjing, China). All proteins were dissolved in 10 mM pH 8.0 Tris-HCl buffer at a concentration of 1 mg mL−1 as stock solutions and diluted to the desired concentration by 10 mM Tris-HCl buffer (pH 8.0). The APT against TM with a sequence of GCCAAAGGAGGCGAGAGATAAAAGATTGCGAATCCATTCG was synthesized by Sangon (Shanghai, China). The enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) kit for TM was purchased from Jiangsu Meimian industrial Co., Ltd (Yancheng, China). Fenneropenaeus chinensis, Trachypenaeus curvirostris, Penaeus vannamei Boone, Macrobrachium nipponese, Metapenaeus ensis, shrimp ball, shrimp dumpling, shrimp wonton, mashed shrimp wah, and shrimp bran were purchased from the local supermarket (Hangzhou, China). Milli-Q water was used to prepare the buffers (Millipore, Massachusetts, USA).

Pure TM was extracted according to the method reported before (Huang et al., 2016). Briefly, the shrimp muscle of Penaeus vannamei Boone was cut into smaller pieces and mixed with ice-cold buffer A (50 mM KCl, 2 mM NaHCO3) and homogenized at 4 °C. After that, the protein slurry was agitated and centrifuged. The precipitates were collected and mixed with ice-cold buffer A. The above steps were repeated 5 times and the precipitates were collected. Precooled acetone was used to wash the precipitates until it was colorless, which was further dried to obtain the crude extract powder. The powder was then treated with ice-cold buffer B (0.2 mM Tris, 1 mM KCl, pH 7.5) and extracted at 4 °C for 2 d. Then, the leaching solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm at 4 °C for 15 min. The obtained supernatant was further purified using the ammonium sulfate precipitation method (Huang et al., 2016). The crude protein solution was eluted using the DEAE Fast Flow column by AKTApure 25 (General Electric Company, Boston, USA). The eluted pure TM and the elutes that did not contain TM (shrimp total protein without TM) were collected during this process. The concentration of the extracted protein was analyzed by BCA assay. The extracted protein was preserved at −80 °C before further experiments.

Fe3O4 magnetic particles were synthesized according to Deng's method (Deng et al., 2005). In brief, 2.7 g of FeCl3·6H2O and 7.2 g of CH3COONa were added in 100 mL C2H6O2 under vigorous stirring. Then the mixture was moved to a Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 8 h. The resultant Fe3O4 magnetic particles were washed 6 times by ethanol and dried under vacuum at room temperature.

20 mg of Fe3O4 particles and 4 mg of TM were dissolved in 4 mL 10 mM pH 8.0 Tris buffer. The solution was shaken for 2 h at 25 °C to fully disperse the Fe3O4 particles. Then 8 mg of dopamine hydrochloride was added and the solution was further shaken for 3 h at 25 °C. After washing thoroughly with Milli-Q water, the Fe3O4@PDA was dissolved in PBS with a final concentration of 5 mg mL−1. Then 0.71 mM EKEKEKEPPPPC polypeptide solution was added to the Fe3O4@PDA at a volume ratio of 10:1 and further incubated at 25 °C with shaking for 24 h. After the reaction, the product was collected by magnetic separation and washed by 3% (v/v) acetic acid solution containing 0.1% (w/v) SDS for five times. Then it was thoroughly washed with Milli-Q water. The control Fe3O4@PDA was prepared by a similar method without addition of the template TM. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the particles were characterized by FEI Tecnai F30 (FEI, USA). The organic element test was conducted by Elementar Vario EL crude (Elementar, Germany) and the Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were collected by the Nicolet iS5 spectrometer (Thermo Nicollet Instruments, Ltd., USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Affinity and specificity of the TM-imprinted Fe3O4@PDA
ce:section.ce:para: For the affinity test, 2 mg of Fe3O4@PDA was suspended in 1.0 mL of TM solution (the solvent was pH 7.5 Tris buffer that containing 0.01% SDS) of different concentrations and incubated at room temperature under shaking for 2 h. The content of TM in the supernatant after magnetic separation was determined by the Bradford kit. The content of TM bound to Fe3O4@PDA was the difference between the total amount of TM and the content of TM in the supernatant. The Scatchard equation was analyzed through the GraphPad Prism 7 (GraphPad Software Inc., San Diego, CA, USA) following the official guide of the software. The specificity test was conducted similarly as the affinity test by comparing the protein binding amount of TM over bovine serum albumin (BSA), shrimp total protein without TM, ovalbumin (OVA), and shrimp arginine kinase (AK) to Fe3O4@PDA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of the fluorescent CD-APT probes
ce:section.ce:para: CD was synthesized according to the previous work (Loo et al., 2016). Briefly, 3 g of malic acid was added in Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated to 205 °C. The temperature was maintained for 15 min after the solid was melted. The mixture was immediately cooled down to room temperature. 0.25 M NaOH was used to adjust the reaction mixture to pH 6.0. Then the mixture was dialyzed in Milli-Q water for 3 days to remove the impurity in the solution. The mixture solution of CD was further concentrated by a vacuum evaporator.

To prepare the fluorescent CD-APT probes, 20 μL CD were mixed with 40 μL 2 μmol L−1 NH2-terminated aptamer and incubated for 2 h at 25 °C. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was recorded by Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (Thermo Scientific, USA). UV–Vis spectra were collected by a UV–Vis spectrometer (Shimadzu UV2600, Kyoto, Japan) and the photoluminescence (PL) spectra were collected by a microplate reader (Spectra Max i3x; Molecular Devices, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of TM by the sandwich biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The sandwich biosensor was constructed by taking the MIP particle as a recognition probe and the CD-APT as a signal probe. Briefly, 2 mg Fe3O4@PDA was mixed with 1 mL TM solution with different concentrations and incubated with shaking for 2 h at 25 °C. The resultant Fe3O4@PDA-TM mixture was washed by 1 mL 0.1 M pH 7.4 phosphate buffer for three times. Then CD-APT probes (APT for 200 pmol) were added into the above Fe3O4@PDA-TM solution and incubated with shaking for 1.5 h at 25 °C. Finally, the TM-biosensor mixture was washed by phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) three times and re-dissolved in 1 mL phosphate buffer (pH 7.4). The fluorescence intensity was measured by a microplate reader.

The TM in five different species of shrimps and five shrimp processed products bought from the local supermarket were analyzed by the sandwich biosensor. The total protein of shrimp samples was collected by a total protein extraction kit from Sangon (Shanghai, China) following the standard procedures and diluted 10 times before quantification. The shrimp processed product samples were prepared by immersing the ground products in buffer A for 6 h at 4 °C. Then the extracts were centrifugated at 4 °C and 10000 rpm for 20 min and the supernatant was collected. The total protein was further analyzed by the SDS-PAGE. The TM contents were measured by the biosensor in a similar method as Section 2.6. For comparison, ELISA was conducted using the commercial kit to evaluate the contents of TM in the shrimp processed product samples. Briefly, 50 μL of the standard TM or the sample was added to the pre-coated ELISA plate and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min. Then each well was washed for 5 times and added with 50 μL of the enzyme reagent. After 30 min incubation at 37 °C, each well was washed for 5 times. Next, 50 μL of chromogen substrate A and 50 μL of the chromogen substrate B were added and further incubated for 10 min at 37 °C in avoidance of light. 50 μL of stop solution was added to stop the reaction and the OD values at 450 nm were measured by the microplate reader.
The statistical significance was analyzed through the one-way analysis of variance by GraphPad prism software 7. All experiments were performed in at least three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and optimization of the TM-imprinted Fe3O4@PDA
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the Fe3O4 particles and Fe3O4@PDA particles were studied by TEM. From Fig. 2 a, it could be seen that the Fe3O4 particles had a uniform size distribution and a PDA layer was observed on the Fe3O4 surface for the Fe3O4@PDA particles. Moreover, FT-IR spectra in Fig. 2b showed that Fe3O4@PDA exhibited an extra benzene ring vibration peak at 1412, 1600 cm−1, and C-N vibration peak at 1090 cm−1 in comparison to the Fe3O4, which further proved the formation of PDA layer on the Fe3O4 surface. In addition, the results of the elemental analysis in Table 1 showed that the nitrogen and carbon contents on the surface were about 5.68% and 5.25%, proving the formation of the PDA layer on the Fe3O4 surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Affinity and specificity of the imprinted Fe3O4@PDA towards TM
ce:section.ce:para: To study the affinity of the imprinted Fe3O4@PDA to TM, their ability in binding TM of different concentrations was studied in comparison to the non-imprinted Fe3O4@PDA. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the TM bound by both the imprinted and control Fe3O4@PDA increased with the enhanced TM concentration. Compared with the control Fe3O4@PDA, the binding amount of the imprinted Fe3O4@PDA was significantly higher, hence, the imprinted Fe3O4@PDA exhibited higher binding ability for TM. Furthermore, the Scatchard analysis (Fig. S1) was used to evaluate the binding ability of the imprinted Fe3O4@PDA. The results showed the dissociation constant of the imprinted Fe3O4@PDA (77.46 μg mL−1) was lower than the control Fe3O4@PDA (353.8 μg mL−1), illustrating that the imprinted Fe3O4@PDA performed a high affinity to TM. Moreover, it was found that the imprinted Fe3O4 particles mostly fitted the one type of binding site for TM.

The specificity of Fe3O4@PDA towards TM was further evaluated in comparison to the nontarget proteins such as BSA, OVA, AK, and shrimp total protein without TM. As shown in Fig. 3b, TM-imprinted Fe3O4@PDA exhibited excellent selectivity in binding TM in respect to the control Fe3O4@PDA. The binding capacity of the imprinted Fe3O4@PDA towards BSA, OVA, AK are lower than that of TM at more than 4.9 times. These results indicated that the imprinted Fe3O4@PDA had high specificity for selective binding of TM. The encouraging affinity and specificity of the imprinted Fe3O4@PDA to TM could result from the multi-point polymerization nature of the dopamine on Fe3O4 particles.

The CD was synthesized by the hydrothermal method using malic acid as the carbon source. As presented in Fig. S2, TEM was used to analyze the morphology of CD. The results demonstrated that the synthesized CD were well dispersed with a round shape. In addition, from the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S3) of the CD, it could be observed that the FT-IR spectra exhibited a broad bending vibration peak which corresponding to the -OH (3380 cm−1), and the peaks at 1720 cm−1 was corresponding to the stretching vibrations of C=O of the carboxylic groups on the CD. Hence, the CD was presented with carboxylic groups. In addition, XPS was used to further observe the elemental composition of the CD. As illustrated in Fig. S4, the carbon-to-oxygen (C/O) ratio of the synthesized CD was 1.56. This suggested that the synthesized CD had a great degree of carbonization (Loo et al., 2016). Three characteristic peaks could be found in the peak fitting curves of the XPS for CD, namely peak of 284.8 eV, 289 eV, and 286.5 eV that corresponded to C-C, O=C-O, and C-OH. These results highlighted the presence of hydrophobic regions and carboxylic moieties on the surface of the synthesized CD. The UV–Vis spectra and fluorescence emission spectra were further used to study the optical nature of the synthesized CD. As Fig. S5 and Fig. S6 showed, the CD exhibited an emission peak around 480 nm and the UV–Vis spectra demonstrated that the synthesized CD showed an absorption peak at 217 nm which was originated from the π-π* transition of aromatic carbon nuclei.

As presented in Fig. S5, the maximum photoluminescence can be obtained at 480 nm when the excitation wavelength of CD-APT probes was 370 nm. Compared to CD, the fluorescence emission spectra of CD-APT probes showed a slight red-shift. Though the fluorescence intensity of the CD-APT was slightly reduced in comparison to CD, it still exhibited a strong emission peak at 480 nm, which proved the great potential of CD-APT as a fluorescent signal probe to detect TM. It could be seen in Fig. S6, the red-shift of the absorption peak was observed in the UV–Vis spectra of the CD-APT probes compared to that of the CD without APT modification. The red-shift in absorption peaks also demonstrated that the APT of TM was successfully conjugated onto the CD.

Detection conditions were optimized to improve the analytical performance of the sandwich biosensor. The influence of the ratio of the Fe3O4@PDA to CD-APT probes was firstly studied. As shown in Fig. 4 a, when the ratio between Fe3O4@PDA and CD-APT was 1:2 and 1:1 (mg/200 pmol APT), the fluorescence intensity exhibited a linear relationship with the concentration of protein but the fluorescence intensity was relatively low. When the ratio was 2:1, the fluorescence intensity was higher than 1:2 and 1:1. This could be due to the increased amount of TM captured by the increased amount of the imprinted Fe3O4@PDA at ratio 2:1. To improve the sensitivity of the detection methods, the optimal ratio of Fe3O4@PDA to CD-APT probes was chosen to be 2:1.

Next, four different detection temperature was explored to optimize the performance of the proposed sandwich biosensors (20 °C, 25 °C, 30 °C, and 37 °C). When the detection temperature was 25 °C, the dose-response curve was y = 22521x+506.6, with the best correlation coefficient (R2 = 0.9879) achieved (Fig. 4b). The linearity of the sandwich biosensor for detection of TM was better at 25 °C than 20 °C, which could be due to the enhanced molecule and particle motions at relatively high temperature and thus increased the recognition probability of the TM by the detection probes. At temperatures above 25 °C, such as those at 30 °C and 37 °C, reduced detection performance was found which might be due to the hampered colloidal stability of particles in the detection system that resulted from the enhanced particle motions at relatively high temperatures. As a better linear relationship could benefit the detection performance of the biosensor, the optimal temperature for the detection process was determined to be 25 °C.

The optimal detection time of the sandwich biosensors was also a critical factor that needed to be explored. As shown in Fig. 4c, four different detection time was explored for the sandwich biosensors (30 min, 60 min, 90 min, and 120 min). It could be seen that the fluorescent signals increased with the extended detection time in 30–90 min, while comparable fluorescent signals could be observed for 90 min and 120 min. It was suggested that the recognition and binding of TM by the signal probes reached a saturation state at 90 min and further increment of the detection time wouldn't significantly enhance the detection signals. Moreover, when the detection time was 90 min, the biosensor was more sensitive and the linear relationship between the TM content and the fluorescence was better than other groups. The dose-response curve was y = 22411x+1556 and the correlation coefficient (R2) was 0.9933. Hence, 90 min was chosen as the optimal detection time for the sandwich biosensors.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Method validation of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: By evaluating the linear range, the limit of detection (LOD), the limit of quantification (LOQ), cross-reactivity, and accuracy of the method, the detection performance of the proposed sandwich biosensor was carefully validated at the optimal conditions determined as above.

Fe3O4@PDA was first incubated with six different concentrations of TM standards (0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 2.5 μg mL−1) to pre-enrich the TM in solution and then further treated with the CD-APT probes to form a sandwich-type complex. The corresponding linear range was displayed in Fig. 5 a. There was a great linear relationship between the concentration of TM and fluorescence intensity when the excitation wavelength of CD-APT probes was 370 nm and the emission wavelength was 480 nm. The dose-response curve was y = 22411x+1556 and the correlation coefficient (R2) was 0.9933. Meanwhile, the limit of detection (LOD) was 30.76 ng mL−1 (S/N = 3) and the limit of quantitation (LOQ) was 102.53 ng mL−1 (S/N = 10). From Table 3
, it could be seen that the proposed method had relatively high sensitivity in comparison to the recently reported methods for TM or other food allergen. Though the detection limit of the proposed method was not as low as these of the ELISA reported by Yu et al. (2019) or chiral biosensor reported by Wang, Li, et al. (2019), it was cost-effective as the molecular imprinting strategy and APT were less expensive than the antibodies. In fact, the labeling threshold for food allergen is determined as 10 ppm of proteins from an allergenic ingredient in food in countries such as Japan (Shoji et al., 2018). Therefore, the proposed method could meet the sensitivity requirements for practical food allergen analysis. Moreover, compared with the chiral biosensor (Wang, Li, et al., 2019) and the surface plasmon resonance (SPR) biosensor (Zhou et al., 2020), the proposed method only required for a common microplate reader and didn't need any specific instruments, indicating its wider applicability.

The specificity of the sandwich biosensors towards TM was further studied over the nontarget proteins such as BSA, OVA, AK, and shrimp proteins without TM. As shown in Fig. 5b, the fluorescence signals given out by the sandwich biosensor for TM were significantly higher than those non-specific proteins. These results illustrated that the sandwich biosensors possessed excellent specificity towards TM.

The accuracy of the sandwich biosensors was verified by the recovery test. TM standards were spiked in shrimp total protein samples with a concentration of 0.1, 0.5, 1.0 μg. As presented in Table 2, the recovery of the sandwich biosensors was between 86.6% and 119.6%, which indicated that the detection accuracy of this method was good.

In order to evaluate the applicability of the sandwich biosensors in detecting TM from the practical seafood samples, the sandwich biosensor was used to quantitatively analyze TM in five shrimp and five shrimp processed products. The total protein extracted from five shrimp and five shrimp processed products were analyzed by SDS-PAGE (Fig. S7). The characteristic bands (36 kDa) of TM were all observed in the different shrimp and shrimp products samples, indicating the existence of the allergen TM in shellfish. The contents of TM in five shrimp were back-calculated from the calibration curves and shown in Table S1, which could supply the information about the TM contents in shrimp of different species. Moreover, the TM contents in the shrimp processed products measured by the proposed biosensor were compared with these measured by the commercial ELISA kit. As shown in Table 4 , the TM contents measured by the biosensor were comparable to these by the ELISA kit, demonstrating that the sandwich biosensor had relatively high reliability in detection of TM from market samples.

In summary, a Fe3O4@PDA and CD-APT probe-based sandwich biosensor were developed to detect TM in seafood products. The proposed sandwich biosensors exhibited a linear quantification range for TM over 0.1–2.5 μg mL−1 with the limit of detection (LOD) as low as 30.76 ng mL−1 (S/N = 3). The recoveries of TM were between 86.6% and 119.6%, which indicated the good accuracy of the method. In addition, the sandwich biosensor was further proved to be efficient in analyzing the TM contents in shrimp-related real samples. These results indicated that the sandwich biosensors could be an accurate and sensitive bioanalysis platform for the determination of TM.

Yanbo Wang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Linfang Li: Investigation, Data curation, Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Huan Li: Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Yafei Peng: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Linglin Fu: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was financially supported by the Zhejiang Provincial Natural Science Foundation of China [grant number LY21C200008], the National Key R&D Program of China [grant number 2019YFC1605004] and Xinmiao Talent Project of Zhejiang Province [grant number 2020R408074].

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
